speaker: interviewer
text: The date is May the 21st, 1996. The survivor is Lili Pohlmann Stern. The interviewer is Sharon Tyler. The city is London, in England, and the language is in English. It's Tuesday, May the 21st, 1996. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing Lili Pohlmann. We're in London, England. Please tell me your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lili Pohlmann, spelled P-O-H-L-M-A-N-N, Lili, L-I-L-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And your name at birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stern, spelled S-T-E-R-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you any other names that you've been known by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My previous married name, which was Cadmon, which was a Hebrew name, spelled C-A-D-M-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please tell me your birth date and age now.

speaker: interviewee
text: 29Th of March, 1930, 66. Terrible thought. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: The city was Lwow, L-W-O-W, which was Poland then, and now it's Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. You were born in Lwow, but your family home, I think, was in-- in Cracow. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something about your early childhood in Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you said, I was born in Lwow, but, immediately afterwards, my parents were in Cracow. And actually, my mother only gave birth to me in Lwow. And we lived in Cracow until the outbreak of war, 1939. The only time I ever went to Lwow was, with my mother, when we were going to my grandparents, in other words, her parents, my mother's parents, when we were going, each year, for Passover. Because they lived in Lwow. Otherwise, it was Cracow for me. And my childhood was-- my childhood literally was spent there. It was a very happy childhood. I attended, through a very early age, a Hebrew kindergarten, which was a rarity, of course. But my-- because my parents were Zionists, very, very ardent Zionists, and their dream was, really, to go to Palestine at the time. And so they decided, when I was born, that they were going to teach me Hebrew, so that I would have no difficulties when we arrived. They both spoke Hebrew. And no problem. And so they found a Hebrew kindergarten. And I was going to that kinder. I was very happy there. And so I was bilingual at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that-- that-- about that nursery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember that much about that kindergarten. I-- I remember a lot of wonderful things. I remember some of the friends I had. Particularly, I remember one boy, who was the grandson of a very prominent man in Cracow. And he was also in the government, actually. And he was a doctor, [? Rozias ?] [? Toen. ?] And his name was [? Gabriel ?] [? Toen, ?] the boy, who was an absolute terror, terror, if there ever was on in the kindergarten. Nobody could cope with him, but because of his prominence, so to speak, there was nothing they could do. They had to keep him. And of course, I was just simply fascinated by [LAUGHS] the boy, because of the things he-- you know and that. He had a gang of boys. And all I wanted to be able to-- to-- to be with them. I was not interested in girls' toys, dolls and things. [SIGHS] And on one occasion, he said to me, alright, you will be able to be with us and do whatever we do, if you promise that you go home, today, and, at lunchtime, when everybody's quietly eating, you will say a word-- and he named a word, which was a nasty, nasty word-- very loudly at the table. I can't do that. All right, so you can't be playing with us. Finished. So I-- I was, of course, disheartened. He said, all right, I'm telling you once more. You will go home, and this is what you do. At the table, you say-- And tomorrow morning, when you come, and if I ask you, have you said it, and if you lie, I want you to know, I'll know it. And of course, I went home, and I shivered. And as we sat at the table and everybody was eating, and, suddenly, I would [CLAP] come out with my word.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the word?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I can't. I mean, it was arse, if you like, but in Polish, of course. And that was a word, you know, children didn't use such words, you know? [LAUGHS] And there was-- there was silence before. But I mean, now, it was-- everybody's, you know, soup were in the spoons-- well. [LAUGHS] So what did-- did-- what my father said, of course, did you say that? And-- and I had to repeat it, what did I say. So I won't go into details what happened at home, but the following morning, when I came, he looked at me. He said, I know it. You said it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, not only once. I had to say it twice. So please, may I now? So I was accepted. And of course, that was fantastic. I-- you know, I mean, with them, we had phenomenal games. My knees were bleeding every day. We were climbing trees, doing all sorts of horrible things. And the poor, poor teachers couldn't cope. And then my parents were called, pretty often, to the kindergarten, could you please do something, this girl is?

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, that didn't last very long, because we moved house. And having moved house, I had to leave this kindergarten. And that was that. I don't know what else you'd like to know. Then, of course, at the age of six, my father decided that it was time I went to school. They did not accept children at the age of six. Seven was the age. But he thought that-- he was of the opinion that if a child can read and write and add 2 and 2 or something like, they should be at school. Well, it was very difficult to get me into a school. And it wasn't just any school. He wanted a specific school, which was a school which gave very strong discipline and a very good education. And it was a school called School Number 1, St. Scholastica, which was a Catholic school. And [SIGHS] there were no Jewish children at that school, being a Catholic school, obviously. But he had his eye on it. And there was no swaying him. And-- and-- and he did manage to get me, even though they-- with all the nos that-- that were on the list, not only starting with my age, but also being Jewish and so forth. That did not [SIGHS] make things easier, put it this way. But he proved to them what he wanted to prove. And they accepted me to school. So I went to that school. It was a very rigoristic school. And it was-- one had to be dressed in a specific fashion, a special type of uniform, on top of which, there was a white overall. And if there was the slightest, slightest stain, one was punished this way or another for it. We had slippers, which were felt lined, so that we weren't-- we weren't heard walking. And also, we wouldn't scratch the beautifully, beautifully shiny parquet floor. And that sort of-- that sort of school, it was. I don't know if I was happy or unhappy in this school. I can't tell. I was going to school, and that was interesting enough. And it wasn't far from home. I walked to school every day on my own. Not something one could easily do today, unfortunately. But I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: On one occasion, some-- we-- I had a very, very unpleasant experience, which stayed in my mind, very vividly. And if you'd like me to tell you about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, it was a Catholic school. And there were three Jewish girls in it, to the best of my knowledge, only. And I was one of them. We heard there was-- there was a recess, smaller recesses after each lesson, and then a larger recess of about 25 minutes at 11:15. And that's when everybody had to leave the classroom, had to take their sandwiches, our breakfast, so to speak-- it was an old-guard school-- outside, in the corridor, sit down and eat. And the classrooms were empty. And I was sent, just before the class finished, the previous class, I was sent by my teacher to bring a note to the headmistress, which I did. And while I was there, the bell went for a recess. And by the time I got to my floor, everybody was already outside of the class. And my breakfast was in the-- in the classroom. I had to go in to get it, which I did. I came out, that was that. The following lesson was a religious-- religion. That meant the priest was coming. And I had to stay outside the classroom, not being Catholic. That was the usual. After that particular lesson, back to the classroom for another lesson.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that lesson was by our class teacher, a Polish lesson. And the moment we sat down, she called me and asks me, why did I go to the classroom during the recess? And I told her, I had to get my breakfast.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, that is not true. I said, but yes, this is why I went. She said, no, no, you are lying to me. You have to own up and tell me why you went to the classroom. I said, this is-- I'm telling you the truth. I went for my breakfast, because you sent me with a note, which caught me in between. And I-- I didn't manage in time.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, you are lying. Come out to me here. And when I did, she said, are you going to tell me the truth now or not? And I said, that's all I can tell you. And she hit me, very, very strongly. She actually hit me in such a manner that I didn't expect it. And I sort of almost fell backwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, now tell me. And I couldn't speak at all. And she hit me again. And blood started pouring and so on. And she said, you came to the classroom, because you wanted to read the sins of-- and she mentioned the name of the girl. And when I say, the sins, it's because, when the priest came for his lesson, the girls had prepared for him what they are about to confess. And they had it, obviously, in their desk, somewhere. And when I went into the class during recess, [SIGHS] I just took my-- whatever I needed to take and went out. And this girl, God knows why, went and reported that I went and I read her sins, which, of course, is a cardinal sin to read somebody else's sins, let alone that, but yet a Jewish girl. And of course, she hit me and beat me up very badly. And it was a very unpleasant incident. I-- slightly-- a slight fracture in my nose. Until today, it's visible. And I went home in that state. My mother, when she saw me, was almost hysterical. And as it happened, my father came home, for lunch, very soon afterwards. And he was-- you know, he was totally stunned over what happened. He thought that I did something coming home, I had an accident. And I told him, because I had a very, very close relationship with my father, much, much more understanding than with my mother. My mother was on a different scale. But with my mother-- with my father, we understood each other very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked me, tell me, do you-- are you telling me the truth now? I want to know the absolute truth from you. Did you or did you not do what? And I said, well, of course I didn't do that. He said, that's enough. You're telling me the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] He went to the-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --he went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the-- the highest authority, which was, you know, an educational authority. And-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --can we interrupt here or not? Mm mm, just carry on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he, of course, related the whole story. And the man, who later became the Minister for Education, but, at that time, he was just over the schools, you know, head of all the schools. And they brought me. And I had to, in front of commissions and God knows what. It was very, very unpleasant for a young child, certainly. And I had to, over and over again, specify, did I did I not. And eventually, eventually, the teacher, who actually admonished all of that, had to apologize to me in front of the class and so on. Because, actually, they-- they-- she was going to be dismissed from school. But if she apologized and so on, openly, which she did. But that was sort of one-- one incident, [SIGHS] which is so vivid. You cannot forget a thing like this, I suppose. But apart from that, I was very happy. I had a very happy childhood. My father was a bank manager. Was-- occasionally, he had to travel, inspections, here and there, in other towns. He liked to take me along. He liked to take me with.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother always, how you can possibly travel with a child? I mean, this is not-- unheard of. He said, yes, I can. Yes, I can. We'll take her nanny. And-- and-- and so we did. And that was, for me, always a wonderful, wonderful time we had together. So in fact, I had a very, very happy childhood. What I remember, also, rather vividly is that when Marshal Pilsudski died, in 1935, it was, I believe-- yes, in May, 1935-- we happened to live in a-- what should I say? It was with an aspect to something, to a place called the Barbican, in Cracow, which is a very auspicious venue and a beautiful sight from the windows.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we were on the third floor, we overlooked, inside, the Barbican and-- and around this square area, around there. And there were all sorts of activities. There were sometimes theatrical performances there. It was an open air, like, today, they-- they open up the Globe Theater, that type, a round, rotund thing. Open and performances of all sorts taking place there. Now, when Marshal Pilsudski died, the-- the funeral procession, which was, of course, a state funeral and so on, was going right under our windows. We could see it all and so on. It was very moving and very resplendent and--

speaker: interviewee
text: But immediately, it was over. The police were beating up the Jews. And from our window, as I said, we had this view and over all of them. I couldn't believe what we were seeing. And I said, well, why are they beating these people? What-- what have they done? Of course, I didn't understand, at the age of five, what have these people have done? Suddenly, they were walking, and the police beating them with truncheons. So it was difficult for my parents to explain to me, that they haven't done anything, nothing. They were just walking. But it was a good opportunity to beat up a few Jews. And that's how it was. And that was sort of representative of the Polish life, do you think, at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much representative of a [SIGHS] I never like to-- to, and I never will say, of the-- of the Polish nation, of-- it's not. It's-- but of a-- of a certain segment, certainly, certainly. At the universities or if you-- if a student was seen with a Jewish girl, it was a good opportunity to beat him up, even if he wasn't Jewish. Well, these things happened. But to me, this was sort of [SIGHS] a very--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that they could not tell me. And I couldn't comprehend. Well, if they haven't done anything, why are they being beaten and so savagely, you know, with-- with-- with the truncheons?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a terrible sight. I think it was the first sight of its kind that I can remember. And that stuck in my memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your Jewish life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very assimilated. I didn't know very much about Jewish life, per se. I-- I mean I knew I was Jewish. I knew my parents wanted to go to Palestine. Because we were Jewish, they wanted to build a Jewish state. All that, I knew. I knew that we were going for Passover to my grandparents. And there, you see, it was quite a different matter, because they were very orthodox. My mother came through a very, very orthodox background. Her father was truly a sort of one of those holy men, who-- who-- who just sit in the synagogue and pray. And that's what he did, all his life, just prayed. And when we came for Passover, [LAUGHS] it was-- the seder night was non-ending. It was an absolutely-- maybe that's how it should be. Or-- I know-- I know seder nights in Israel are quite different. But to me, it was, again, a shock to my system, when I, years and years later, went to-- to an Israeli Seder. And I thought-- I thought, I don't think that is right. This is not the right sort of seder, because I was so accustomed to the way my grandfather. You know, [HEBREW] which means-- you know what it means. I mean, semi-reclining and-- and taking every word, you know, chanting every word from the Haggadah. That was, you know. So on one hand, I had this. And my mother came from this background. But my father was very assimilated. He-- absolutely nothing religious for him. So I was brought up in this vein. I-- every Friday, my grandmother would send parcels, which would arrive every Friday. She would bake all sorts of lovely things for Shabbat-- and-- or for her, for Shabbos. And-- and that arrived, religiously, every-- every Friday, to have it on the table, a challah, which she made herself, and all that. When I think about it, today, it's almost unheard of that-- that one can do it, that one can send it by post, and it arrives fresh. [LAUGHS] It's-- it's unbelievable. But she-- she used to do every Friday. And my mother, on the other hand, used to write a letter in Yiddish to her parents, so that they get it for Shabbos. They get a good Shabbos letter and so on. That was-- must have been a great conflict for her, because although she was-- she came from such a home, but she, herself, was not orthodox. She was, as I said, a Zionist, young person, who wanted to-- to go and build the land and so, which was totally against, if I may say so, the religion of-- of-- of her parents. And it must have been conflicting. But in my home, where my father was, we had no tradition. I mean Jewish, traditional, but not religious in the aspect of going to synagogues and so forth and all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the conflict didn't seem to affect your immediate family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just maybe your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. I would never have known. I mean, I don't know what would have happened later. Maybe we-- if-- if-- should-- should we have lived, all of us, and-- and-- if I had been older, how that would have been, I don't know. But certainly not at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a-- had a brother, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much-- what was his age and tell me about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born-- he was born in December, 1935. He was four and a half years younger than I. And he was quite the opposite to me. I was dark. He was fair. I was like my father. He was like my mother in every aspect. He was very delicate, very gentle, very, very warm, quiet, highly, highly intelligent, a very, very clever child, a very clever child. A child who, during the war, taught himself all sorts of things at the very tender age of four and five and six-- not quite six. And he played chess, magnificently. We didn't know about it. When I say, we, I mean my parents didn't know about it at all. He taught himself Russian. We didn't know about it. He could read the paper. We did know about it. I mean that sort of thing. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he do them secretly?

speaker: interviewee
text: --was quite extraordinary. I don't know. Out of interest. How did we learn that he knew it? It was-- everything was per chance. It was because, one day-- now I'm going a little further. I'm going to the Russian occupation in Lwow. But there were a lot of people coming to-- to visit, because they were-- they were on their own and sort of escaped like we did. And on one such occasion, there were a number of people around the table. And this child, the boy, took a Russian newspaper and-- and sort of opened it up. And we couldn't see him. The paper was huge. And-- and-- and he was small. And we couldn't see him. He was behind that paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, somebody says to him, Uri, why-- what are you doing, there, behind this paper? He says, I'm reading. How are you reading? Everybody laughing, of course. Ha ha, you are reading the paper. So read something to us. So he proceeded to read. And you know, we were-- everybody was so stunned, totally stunned, because unbelievable. Uri, are you actually reading? So somebody took the paper to see, yes, he was reading the paper. How did you learn? Who taught you? I taught myself. And-- and so forth, and so, you know, whatever he did, he did that way. He was very clever.
